topics: 💰 Theft of partnership investment — סלעים גסות vs. אבנים דקות
🐂 Mu'ad to its kind / to humans / to small ones — selective notoriety
📅 R' Yehudah: a bull can be מועד only on Shabbatot
🩸 Niddah parallel: when intermittent goring counts (Rav Ada vs. Rav Huna)
🛕 שור ישראל / שור הקדש / שור נכרי — gentile and Temple oxen
👑 Rabban Gamliel and the two Roman officials — חילול השם

overview.title: 📖 Overview — Selective Notoriety and Whose Ox Is Liable
overview.label: The sugya:
overview.text: Daf 19 covers a rich stretch. It opens with a partnership-loss puzzle — when investment items are stolen, do all partners share the loss equally or proportionally? It then moves to the Mishnah of selective mu'ad: an ox notorious for goring its own kind but not other species, humans but not animals, small ones but not large ones, weekdays vs. Shabbat. The Yerushalmi links this to a parallel niddah debate about non-continuous bleeding. Finally the daf turns to oxen of hekdesh and of gentiles, ending with the dramatic story of the two Roman officials sent to study Torah with Rabban Gamliel.
overview.hebrewSource: שׁוֹר שֶׁהוּא מוּעָד לְמִינוֹ וְאֵינוֹ מוּעָד לְשֶׁאֵינוֹ מִינוֹ... אֵימָתַי הוּא תָּם מִשֶּׁיַּחֲזוֹר בּוֹ שְׁלֹשֶׁת יְמֵי שַׁבָּתוֹת

slides.title: 🪙 Stolen Coins vs. Stolen Stones
slides.subtitle: How a partnership absorbs theft depends on what was stolen.

boxes.title: 🟡 סְלָעִים גַּסּוֹת (large coins)
items: אֶיפְשַׁר לִבְלוֹל לָצֵאת יְדֵי כוּלָּן — possible to subdivide exactly
Loss can be split cleanly proportional to investment

boxes.title: 💎 אֲבָנִים דַּקּוֹת (small stones)
items: לֵית יְדַע אִי מִן הָדֵין נְסַב אִי מִן הָדֵין נְסַב

boxes.title: ⚖️ R' Yose b. R' Bun's worry
items: Equal-loss rule lets the small investor lose nothing
נִמְצֵאת מִידַּת הַדִּין לוֹקָה — equity is hurt

callout.label: The technical move:
callout.text: R' Shammai distinguishes objects that can be re-tallied (coins) from objects that can't (small stones). Where re-tallying fails, the doubt itself reshapes the rule.

slides.title: 🐂 Selective Mu'ad — The Mishnah's Categories
slides.subtitle: An ox can be notorious for one target and tame for another.

nodes.content: שור שהוא מועד למינו ואינו מועד לשאינו מינו

Damage to the target it is מועד for → נזק שלם
Damage to a different target → חצי נזק
Reverts to תם only after שְׁלֹשֶׁת יְמֵי שַׁבָּתוֹת of changed behavior

callout.label: Yerushalmi's question:
callout.text: Why would Shabbat make a difference? מִכֵּיוָן דּוּ חֲמִי לוֹן לְבִישִׁין נְקִיִין עוֹד הוּא מְשַׁנֶּה דַּעְתֵּיהּ — when the ox sees people in clean clothes, it changes its behavior.

slides.title: 🩸 The Niddah Parallel — Rav Ada vs. Rav Huna
slides.subtitle: Can three goring incidents on three different days make one mu'ad?

lines.speaker: Q
lines.text: What if the ox gored on day 1, was kept in on day 2, and gored on day 3? Does the unattested middle day break the chazakah?

lines.speaker: RA
lines.text: Rav Ada b. Achwa quoting Rav: parallel to the niddah who checked, found tamei, skipped a day, then found tamei again — נִדָּה וַדַּאי. Definitely a niddah.

lines.speaker: RH
lines.text: Rav Huna quoting Rav: נִדָּה סָפֵק. Doubtful. The gap matters.

lines.speaker: RH+
lines.text: Rav Huna explains: I was there at the beginning, middle, and end of the conversation. Rav first said certain, reversed himself, ended with safek. Rav Ada only heard the first answer.

callout.label: Why the parallel works:
callout.text: Both cases ask the same structural question: does an unattested middle day break a presumptive chazakah? The Yerushalmi imports the niddah debate to illuminate the goring case.

slides.title: 🛕 Whose Ox Triggers Liability
slides.subtitle: The Mishnah's third halakhah maps liability across owner categories.

callout.label: The aggadot behind the law:
callout.text: Rav: רָאָה וַיַּתֵּר גּוֹיִם — He saw and 'permitted' the gentiles, i.e., released their property to those they oppressed. R' Yose b. Chaninah: He impoverished them. R' Yochanan (via R' Abbahu): כְּדִינֵיהֶן — by their own laws.

slides.title: 👑 The Two Roman Officials and Rabban Gamliel
slides.subtitle: A daf-ending story with serious halakhic teeth.

steps.content: 1️⃣ The Roman government sent two איסרטיוטות (officials) to study Torah with Rabban Gamliel.
2️⃣ They learned Mikra, Mishnah, Talmud, halakhot, and aggadot.
3️⃣ At the end they objected to three rulings: midwifery, nursing, and stolen property of a non-Jew.
4️⃣ Specifically the rule that שׁוֹר שֶׁלְּיִשְׂרָאֵל שֶׁנָּגַח לְשׁוֹר שֶׁלַּנָּכְרִי פָּטוּר.

5️⃣ Rabban Gamliel decreed that gezel akum is forbidden מִפְּנֵי חִילּוּל הָשֵׁם — to prevent desecration of God's Name. Yet by the time the officials reached the Tyrian ladder, they had forgotten everything.

callout.label: The story's punch:
callout.text: Halakhah is reshaped under the gaze of outsiders. Rabban Gamliel does not deny what is taught; he tightens the practical rule so the Torah will not be desecrated. The miraculous forgetting then preserves the original Mishnaic letter.

summary.principle: An ox can be selectively notorious — by species, person, size, or day — and the rules of how a chazakah forms or breaks parallel the niddah debate. Liability also depends on whose ox is involved, and the daf closes by softening the gentile-ox rule for the sake of God's Name.

flowSteps.content:
Selective mu'ad
שור מועד למינו ואינו מועד לשאינו מינו

Three goring incidents establish the chazakah — but what about a gap day?
Niddah parallel: ודאי or ספק?

columns.content:
👑 R' Gamliel
גזל גוי אסור מפני חילול השם

flowSteps.content:
🎓 Key Principle:
Notoriety is target-specific, chazakah depends on continuity, and even halakhot anchored in pesukim get reshaped to prevent חילול השם.

quiz.q: Why does the Yerushalmi distinguish stolen סלעים גסות from stolen אבנים דקות?
o: Because coins are mu'ad and stones are tam
Because large coins can be subdivided exactly while small stones cannot, so doubt requires each partner to take by his purse
Because stones are forbidden as collateral
Because R' Eleazar disqualifies stones
quiz.e: R' Shammai: סלעים are large enough to subdivide exactly. With small stones, לֵית יְדַע אִי מִן הָדֵין נְסַב, so each takes proportional to his investment.

quiz.q: What does R' Yehudah rule about a bull mu'ad on Shabbatot but not weekdays?
o: Always pays full damage
Always pays half damage
Pays full damage on Shabbat, half on weekdays — and reverts to תם only after three Shabbat days of changed behavior
Such a category does not exist
quiz.e: בַּשַּׁבָּתוֹת מְשַׁלֵּם נֶזֶק שָׁלֵם וּבִימוֹת הַחוֹל מְשַׁלֵּם חֲצִי נֶזֶק, and reverting requires שלשת ימי שבתות.

quiz.q: What is the Yerushalmi's tentative reason for R' Yehudah's Shabbat-only rule?
o: Shabbat law overrides nezikin
מִכֵּיוָן דּוּ חֲמִי לוֹן לְבִישִׁין נְקִיִין עוֹד הוּא מְשַׁנֶּה דַּעְתֵּיהּ — seeing people in clean clothes, the ox changes its behavior
Sabbaths have shorter days
The owner is more careful on Shabbat
quiz.e: The Yerushalmi suggests the ox is reacting to the visual cue of clean Shabbat clothing.

quiz.q: What does Rav Huna report about the niddah-with-a-gap question?
o: Rav definitely said וודאי
Rav definitely said ספק
Rav first said וודאי, reversed himself, and finally said ספק; Rav Ada only heard the first answer
It was never decided
quiz.e: Rav Huna says he was at the entire conversation: ברישא, באמצעיתא, ובסיפא, and Rav landed on ספק.

quiz.q: Why did Rabban Gamliel decree that robbing a non-Jew is forbidden, given the Mishnah's exemption rule?
o: He rejected the Mishnah
Because of חִילּוּל הָשֵׁם after the two Roman officials objected
Because of a kal va-chomer from שור הקדש
Because the Romans paid him to change it
quiz.e: בְּאוֹתָהּ שָׁעָה גָּזַר רַבָּן גַּמְלִיאֵל עַל גְּזֵילוֹת נָכְרִי שֶׁיְּהֵא אָסוּר מִפְּנֵי חִילּוּל הָשֵׁם. The rule was tightened so Torah would not be desecrated.
